title: Dunning-Kruger Effect
definition: A cognitive bias where people with low ability at a task overestimate their ability (illusory superiority), while people with high ability underestimate their competence (impostor syndrome). The effect is driven by metacognitive deficits: the unskilled lack the expertise to recognize their incompetence.

proofs.description: Multiple studies across domains (logic, grammar, humor) show bottom quartile performers overestimate rank; top quartile underestimate
proofs.reference: Kruger, J., & Dunning, D. (1999). Unskilled and Unaware of It. Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 77(6), 1121-1134.

difficulty_levels.beginner: People who are bad at something often think they're good at it. People who are good often think they're worse than they really are.
difficulty_levels.intermediate: The Dunning-Kruger effect is a metacognitive bias: incompetent individuals lack the ability to recognize their own incompetence, while competent individuals assume others are equally competent, leading to underestimation.
difficulty_levels.expert: The effect arises from dual deficits: poor performers lack both skill and the metacognitive ability to evaluate skill. Statistical regression effects and idiosyncratic task difficulty contribute. Debates continue about effect magnitude and whether it's a statistical artifact.

extra_fields.historical_context: First systematically studied by David Dunning and Justin Kruger at Cornell University (1999). Winner of the 2000 Ig Nobel Prize in Psychology.
extra_fields.limitations: Effect size varies; some studies show only weak or inconsistent effects. Statistical artifacts may explain some findings.
